scenario: A logistics process tracks package handoff between three internal teams: Receiving, Sorting, Dispatch. Each team is a separate organisational unit but the process is one BPMN file. Management wants visual swim-lane separation so each team sees their own tasks immediately, while the overall flow remains a single process.
question: Which BPMN construct fits this requirement?

options.text: Three separate Pools â€” one per team â€” connected by Message Flows represent distinct BPMN participants, each compiling into its own Zeebe process definition with a separate lifecycle. For three internal teams sharing one logistics process, this creates unnecessary messaging overhead and multi-definition complexity in Operate. The overall flow is no longer a single process definition â€” it becomes three coordinated but separate processes with independent deployment units.

Single Pool with three Lanes â€” one Lane per team â€” is the canonical BPMN construct for visually separating responsibilities within a single process. In Camunda 8, one Pool equals one Zeebe process definition. Lanes are purely visual; they carry no execution semantics but clearly identify which team owns which tasks. Tasks in the Receiving, Sorting, or Dispatch Lane are immediately recognizable as that team work, satisfying visual swim-lane separation without splitting into multiple deployable units.

Single Pool with no Lanes, using Task documentation fields to indicate team ownership, keeps the process unified but fails the visual swim-lane separation requirement. Documentation strings in BPMN elements are only visible when the modeler clicks into element properties â€” they are never rendered on the diagram canvas. Management cannot glance at the diagram and identify team boundaries instantly. This approach hides organisational context rather than surfacing it visually as swim-lane separation requires.

Three separate BPMN files chained via Call Activities is a reuse pattern for independently governed subprocesses shared across many parents. For three internal teams on a single logistics handoff, this creates disproportionate overhead: three BPMN versions to maintain, three definitions to monitor in Operate, and inter-process variable passing via mappings. The single-Pool-with-Lanes approach achieves the same visual separation at a fraction of the operational cost for what is fundamentally one connected process. ###

a.text: Incorrect. Lanes are the canonical BPMN construct for visual segregation within a single process. One Pool = one Zeebe process definition; multiple Lanes = visual ownership swim-lanes. Tasks placed in the Sorting Lane are clearly "Sorting's work" without breaking the single-process flow.

b.text: Correct. Multiple Pools represent separate participants that communicate via Message Flows -- not internal teams of one organisation. Each Pool becomes its own process definition.

c.text: Incorrect. Documentation strings aren't visible without inspecting; defeats the "visual swim-lane separation" requirement.

d.text: Incorrect. Three BPMN files for internal team handoffs adds deployment/versioning overhead with no benefit.

explanation: Multiple Pools represent separate participants that communicate via Message Flows -- not internal teams of one organisation. Each Pool becomes its own process definition.
